name: Fort Campbell
tagline: Home of the 101st Airborne Division (Air Assault), 5th Special Forces Group, and 160th SOAR.
_note: Seed record. Killer-content (two-state decision) is drafted/real; other sections are structural stubs pending verified content (see MilHousing-Base-Guide-Schema-v0.1).

sections.title: Mission & Identity
data: Fort Campbell straddles the Kentucky-Tennessee border and is one of the Army's premier power-projection platforms, home to the 101st Airborne Division (Air Assault).
It also hosts significant special-operations forces, including the 5th Special Forces Group and the 160th Special Operations Aviation Regiment (SOAR).

sections.title: Tennessee or Kentucky? The Two-State Decision

data.subtitle: For buyers and renters PCSing to Campbell, the side of the line you choose is a several-thousand-dollar decision over a typical tour.
intro: Fort Campbell sits on the Kentucky-Tennessee state line. The post office address is in Kentucky, but most of the housing, most of the gates, and the largest off-post community (Clarksville) sit on the Tennessee side. That geography forces a decision other military families never have to make: which state to live in.

The two states tax differently, school children differently, and value homes differently. Over a typical three-to-four-year tour the cumulative difference between Clarksville and Hopkinsville or Oak Grove adds up to thousands of dollars - plus a meaningfully different schooling and real-estate experience.

recommendations.who: Buyers with school-age kids, 3-4 yr tour
recommendations.advice: Default to Clarksville (Sango or Rossview): stronger schools, faster resale, tax math roughly a wash.
Look hard at Hopkinsville - noticeably more house per budget; plan for a slower sale at next PCS.
Oak Grove - base proximity is unmatched.
Tennessee (Clarksville) for the schools; the tax advantage compounds less over a short lease.
